prediction.interfaces.idx_0.phase_evolution: In alloys with 1% C, 10% Al, and about 10% Mn the γ-phase decomposes into α-phase and carbides after quenching + aging at 600°C. With increasing Mn content, retained austenite increases and a new brittle Mn-like phase appears in grain boundaries.

truth.characterization_methods.sample_25mn_10a: dispersed phase in grain boundaries, higher etchability of grain boundaries (Fig.2,b)
truth.characterization_methods.sample_20mn_10a: austenite, ferrite, carbides in grain boundaries, acicular austenite around ferrite (Fig.2,b,II)
truth.characterization_methods.sample_30mn_10a: brittle phase has a crystal lattice similar to manganese

prediction.characterization_methods.idx_0.purpose: Microstructure observation after quenching and aging
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_0.key_findi: Observed single-phase gamma, two-phase alpha+gamma, carbides at grain boundaries, dispersed phases, and brittle Mn-like phase depending on composition and treatment.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_30mn_10a: The new brittle phase in the alloy with 30% Mn has a crystal lattice similar to manganese.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_27_5mn9_: Mechanical properties measured on samples 5 mm in diameter and 25 mm long after quenching and aging 16 h at different temperatures.
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_3.purpose: Magnetic property evaluation after quenching and aging
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_3.key_findi: Alloys with austenitic structure after quenching are completely nonmagnetic; permeability increases after aging, especially in alloys with over 10% Al.
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_4.key_findi: Weight increase measured after 500 h at 600, 700, and 800°C. Oxidation resistance depends mainly on aluminum concentration.
prediction.characterization_methods.idx_5.key_findi: Density decreases with increasing aluminum concentration from 6 to 10% Al.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Over 100 heats were prepared with 5-45% Mn, 2-14% Al, 0.05-1.5% C; melting in laboratory induction furnace under helium atmosphere or 30-kg commercial furnace; samples produced by investment casting: 65 mm long, 11 mm diameter.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Aging at 550-600°C causes intense precipitation of dispersed phases, while at 400-500°C the amount precipitated is negligible.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: In alloys with ~9% Al (30% Mn) ultimate and yield strengths increase with aging temperature up to 550°C, then decrease above 550°C due to coalescence of dispersed phase; alloys with 12-14% Al have low plasticity both after quenching and aging.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Alloys with austenitic structure after quenching are completely nonmagnetic; permeability increases after aging, especially in alloys with >10% Al, probably due to precipitation of iron-aluminum carbides.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Oxidation resistance tested in air at 600, 700, 800°C; after 500 h at 700°C weight increase was 6-12.5 g/m² for alloys with >8% Al, compared to 6.2 g/m² for steel 1Kh17N2 and 118 g/m² for steel 20KhM.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Density determined by hydrostatic suspension; with increasing Al from 6 to 10% density decreased from 7.24 to 6.68 g/cm³; alloys with 9% Al and 25-30% Mn had density 6.75-6.85 g/cm³.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Figure 1 shows the iron corner of the Fe-Mn-Al phase diagram with ~1% C at 1150°C; alloys below line ab have single-phase γ, above line two-phase α+γ.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Carbon influences structure: alloy with 30% Mn, 10% Al has single-phase α at ~0.05% C, two-phase α+γ at 0.25% C, and single-phase γ at 1% C after quenching.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Table 1 may contain additional mechanical property data for quenched+aged 593°C (G30Yu9) but values were not present in the extracted text; only quenched state values are recorded.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: The units for δ and ψ in Fig.3 caption were originally listed as kg/mm² but are likely % (assumed % in extraction).

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Figure 1 shows the iron corner of the Fe-Mn-Al phase diagram with approximately 1% C at 1150°C, distinguishing single-phase gamma-solid solution alloys from two-phase gamma-plus-alpha alloys.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Figure 2 provides micrographs of alloys with 25% Mn, 10% Al, 1% C (I) and 20% Mn, 10% Al, 1% C (II) after quenching from 1150°C and after quenching plus aging at 600°C for 50 h; pure text pipeline has no image pixels.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Figure 3 shows mechanical properties of the alloy with 27.5% Mn, 9.1% Al, and 0.92% C in relation to aging temperature (16 h), presenting sigma_b, sigma_0.2, psi, and delta values at 450, 500, and 550°C.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Table 1 lists mechanical properties for alloy G30Yu9 in the quenched condition (sigma_b = 84 kg/mm^2, sigma_0.2 = 57 kg/mm^2, delta = 72%, psi = 70%) but numerical values for the aged conditions and for the alloy with 26.1% Mn, 10.2% Al, 1.02% C are not visible in the provided table snippet.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Table 2 reports magnetic permeability (G/Oe) of five specific alloy compositions after quenching and after quenching plus aging 20 h at 600°C; specific values range from 1.007 to 1.038 (quenched) and 1.040 to 3.860 (aged), with the highest aged permeability of 3.860 for 0.97% C, 30.5% Mn, 10.6% Al.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: The alloy with 25% Mn, 10% Al, 1% C exhibited a sharp hardness increase after aging at 600°C from HRB 86-88 to HRC 35-37.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Alloys with about 10% Al obtain optimal mechanical properties by aging at 475-500°C, while aging at 550-575°C substantially reduces ductility and strength.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Alloys with 12-14% Al have low plasticity and ductility both after quenching and after aging.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: The oxidation resistance after 500 h at 700°C in air showed a weight increase of 6-12.5 g/m^2 for alloys with over 8% Al, compared to 6.2 g/m^2 for steel 1Kh17N2 and 118 g/m^2 for boiler steel 20KhM.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Density measurements show that with increasing aluminum from 6% to 10%, density decreases from 7.24 to 6.68 g/cm^3; alloys with 9% Al and 25-30% Mn have a density of 6.75-6.85 g/cm^3.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Machinability of these Fe-Mn-Al alloys is similar to that of 18/8 steels, and they can be processed by hot and cold working.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: Over 100 heats were prepared in the composition range 5-45% Mn, 2-14% Al, and 0.05-1.5% C, melted in a laboratory induction furnace under helium or in a 30-kg commercial furnace.
